text: The latter part of this verse seem out of place, and think the Hebrew text has been incorrectly transmitted. A very slight alteration would give the sense, ‘The cities of Syria belong to the Lord, just as the tribes of Israel’.

Some think this verse implies that the Philistines will be converted to Jewish customs, abstaining from meat with blood in it and other forbidden food. But it may be Philistia is compared to a beast of prey; cf. Am. 3.12.
Literally, ‘a chieftain’, but this makes the sense difficult; some scholars, by a different pointing of the Hebrew text, would read ‘a clan’.
Literally, ‘and a colt, the foal of an ass’; the implication seems to be that the king will come in less than royal state. See Mt. 21.5.
Literally, ‘And the Lord their God will deliver them in that day, like the flock of his people, because (they shall be like) stones of sanctification that are lifted up as a standard over his land’.
‘A people how blessed and how fair’; literally, ‘What is the goodness of it and what is the beauty of it!’ Or possibly ‘of him’, but the word ‘beauty’ would be out of place in this context.
The reference of verses 11-17 is obscure; the conflict between Jewish and Greek culture did not make itself felt until nearly three hundred years after Zachary’s time.
